title: Learning to See: Composition for People Who Overthink It
subtitle: Rules are a starting point, not a cage
excerpt: The rule of thirds is the first thing every beginner learns and the first thing every good photographer quietly breaks. Here is how I stopped overthinking the frame.
content:
When I started, composition felt like a test I was failing in real time. Rule of thirds, leading lines, negative space — I had the vocabulary but my photos still felt flat. What changed was treating these as questions rather than rules.

Instead of asking "is this on the thirds?" I started asking "where do I want the eye to land, and what is getting in its way?" That single reframing did more for my photos than any tutorial. Suddenly negative space was not an abstract idea — it was just removing the clutter that pulled attention away from the subject.

My current mental checklist before pressing the shutter:

  1. What is the subject? If I cannot answer in one word, the frame is too busy.
  2. What is the eye doing? Lines, contrast, and focus should all point the same way.
  3. What can I remove? Almost always, the answer is "something."

Breaking the rules on purpose

Dead-centre composition is supposedly a beginner mistake. But for symmetrical subjects it is the only thing that feels right. The rules are training wheels — useful until the moment they are not.

Learn the rules so well that breaking them feels like a decision, not an accident.

Composition stopped being a source of anxiety the day I accepted I would get it wrong a lot. Photography is the one hobby where failing a hundred times a day is completely normal.
